title: Why we save things we'll never use
summary: Reflect on the habit of keeping objects, files, or materials that serve no practical purpose, and explore what this reveals about us.
topic: Why we save things we'll never use
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (350–500 words) reflecting on why people hold on to things they will never use again. Describe a specific object — your own or someone else's — that has been kept without reason. Use present perfect, modal verbs, and at least one sentence with 'although' or 'however'.
hintStarters: The strangest thing I've ever kept is…
What strikes me about this habit is that it isn't really about the object itself, but…
If I threw everything away that I hadn't used in a year, I would…

hintVocab.meaningVi: chia tay, từ bỏ thứ gì
bám bụi, không được dùng đến
có ích vào một lúc nào đó
lần lượt xem qua và phân loại
đã hết giá trị sử dụng
một ngăn kéo đầy dây cáp

sampleText:
My uncle has kept a fax machine in his spare room in Warsaw since 1997. He has not used it once in twenty years, but every time my aunt suggests throwing it away, he finds a reason to keep it. 'You never know,' he says, which is arguably the most powerful argument any hoarder has ever made. The machine has gathered dust through four apartments, two children, and a complete change of every other piece of technology in his life. It has clearly outlived its purpose, and yet it stays.

What strikes me about this habit is that it isn't really about the object. My uncle doesn't need a fax machine; he needs to feel that he has a backup plan. Objects become proxies for readiness, for competence, for a version of yourself that might one day return. The same logic explains the drawer full of cables that don't match any current device, the half-finished jar of spice from a recipe you made once in 2019, the dress that no longer fits but which felt important at the time you bought it. Although we know these things serve no practical purpose, we feel that discarding them is a small act of pessimism — an admission that the future we imagined for them will not arrive.

However, the real cost of keeping things we'll never use is not the space they occupy. It is the low-level noise they add to our surroundings. Every object we own makes a small claim on our attention; things we've chosen not to let go make a slightly larger one. I have sorted through the belongings of people who have died and found boxes of receipts from the 1980s, plugs with no appliance, clothes in sizes they hadn't worn for decades. Their families couldn't part with them either, at least not immediately, because by then the objects had absorbed so much time that throwing them away felt like throwing away the person. That, I think, is the clearest explanation of why we save things: not because we might use them, but because we're not yet ready to admit that a chapter is closed.

sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: bám bụi, không được dùng đến
đã hết giá trị sử dụng
đại diện cho sự sẵn sàng
một ngăn kéo đầy dây cáp
tiếng ồn mức thấp, sự xao nhãng âm thầm

sampleAnnotations.phrase: makes a small claim on our attention
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: chiếm một phần nhỏ sự chú ý của chúng ta
lần lượt xem qua đồ vật
không thể chia tay với chúng
đã hấp thụ quá nhiều thời gian
việc vứt bỏ chúng có cảm giác như
không phục vụ mục đích thực tế nào

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Present perfect to emphasise duration
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'He has not used it once in twenty years' dùng hiện tại hoàn thành phủ định để nhấn mạnh rằng điều gì đó chưa xảy ra trong suốt một khoảng thời gian dài kéo dài đến hiện tại.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'He has not used it once in twenty years' uses the negative present perfect to emphasise that something has not happened across a long period stretching up to the present.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Concession with 'although'
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Although we know these things serve no practical purpose, we feel that discarding them is a small act of pessimism' — dùng 'although' để thừa nhận một thực tế trước khi đưa ra lý giải mâu thuẫn nhưng thực tế hơn.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Although we know these things serve no practical purpose, we feel that discarding them is a small act of pessimism' — 'although' acknowledges a fact before introducing a contradictory but more emotionally real explanation.

sampleTranslationVi:
Chú tôi đã giữ một máy fax trong phòng trống của mình ở Warsaw từ năm 1997. Ông ấy chưa dùng nó một lần nào trong hai mươi năm, nhưng mỗi khi cô tôi đề nghị vứt đi, ông lại tìm ra lý do để giữ lại. 'Biết đâu lại cần,' ông nói, đây có thể nói là lý lẽ mạnh nhất mà bất kỳ người tích trữ nào từng đưa ra. Chiếc máy đã bám bụi qua bốn căn hộ, hai đứa con, và sự thay đổi hoàn toàn của mọi công nghệ khác trong cuộc đời ông. Nó rõ ràng đã hết giá trị sử dụng, thế mà vẫn còn đó.

Điều làm tôi ấn tượng về thói quen này là nó không thực sự liên quan đến đồ vật. Chú tôi không cần máy fax; ông cần cảm giác rằng mình có kế hoạch dự phòng. Đồ vật trở thành đại diện cho sự sẵn sàng, cho năng lực, cho một phiên bản của bản thân mà có thể một ngày nào đó sẽ quay lại. Cùng một logic giải thích ngăn kéo đầy dây cáp không khớp với bất kỳ thiết bị hiện tại nào, lọ gia vị làm dở từ một công thức bạn nấu một lần vào năm 2019, chiếc váy không còn vừa nữa nhưng cảm thấy quan trọng vào thời điểm bạn mua nó. Mặc dù chúng ta biết những thứ này không phục vụ mục đích thực tế nào, chúng ta cảm thấy rằng vứt bỏ chúng là một hành động bi quan nhỏ — một sự thừa nhận rằng tương lai ta tưởng tượng cho chúng sẽ không đến.

Tuy nhiên, chi phí thực sự của việc giữ những thứ ta sẽ không bao giờ dùng không phải là không gian chúng chiếm. Đó là tiếng ồn mức thấp mà chúng thêm vào môi trường xung quanh ta. Mỗi đồ vật ta sở hữu tạo ra một yêu cầu nhỏ về sự chú ý của ta; những thứ ta chọn không buông bỏ tạo ra yêu cầu lớn hơn một chút. Tôi đã lần lượt xem qua đồ vật của những người đã mất và tìm thấy hộp biên lai từ những năm 1980, phích cắm không có thiết bị đi kèm, quần áo theo kích cỡ họ không mặc trong nhiều thập kỷ. Gia đình họ cũng không thể chia tay với chúng, ít nhất là không ngay lập tức, vì lúc đó các đồ vật đã hấp thụ quá nhiều thời gian đến mức việc vứt bỏ chúng có cảm giác như vứt bỏ người đó. Đó, theo tôi nghĩ, là lý giải rõ ràng nhất về lý do chúng ta giữ lại đồ vật: không phải vì chúng ta có thể dùng chúng, mà vì chúng ta chưa sẵn sàng thừa nhận rằng một chương đã khép lại.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the present perfect negative correctly?
options: He has not used it once in twenty years.
He have not used it once in twenty years.
He has not use it once in twenty years.
He not has used it once in twenty years.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect negative = has/have + not + past participle; third-person singular takes 'has'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Has not' (or 'hasn't') + past participle for third-person singular.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option correctly uses 'although' to make a concession?
options: Although we know these things serve no purpose, we feel discarding them is pessimistic.
Although we know these things, but we feel discarding them is pessimistic.
Although, we know these things serve no purpose we feel discarding them is pessimistic.
We know these things serve no purpose although, we feel discarding them is pessimistic.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + clause, main clause' — no extra 'but'; no comma directly after 'although'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Although' already signals contrast — don't add 'but' after the concessive clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'however' correctly to introduce a contrast?
options: However, the real cost is not the space they occupy.
However the real cost is not the space they occupy.
The real cost, however is not the space they occupy.
The real cost is not, however the space they occupy.
mcQuestions.explanation: When 'however' starts a new sentence as a discourse marker, it is followed immediately by a comma.
mcQuestions.hint: A sentence-initial 'however' needs a comma right after it.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a modal to express possibility?
options: Objects become proxies for a version of yourself that might one day return.
Objects become proxies for a version of yourself that might one day to return.
Objects become proxies for a version of yourself that might one day returning.
Objects become proxies for a version of yourself that might one day returned.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Might + bare infinitive' expresses possibility; no 'to', no '-ing', no past form after 'might'.
mcQuestions.hint: Modal verbs are followed by the bare infinitive (no 'to').

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase correctly describes something that has lost its usefulness?
options: outlived its purpose
outlived its use
over-lived its purpose
out-used its purpose
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Outlive its purpose' is the natural idiom for something that has existed beyond the reason for keeping it.
mcQuestions.hint: This is a fixed phrase — only one verb collocates with 'its purpose' in this sense.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a relative clause to define a noun correctly?
options: The dress that no longer fits but which felt important at the time you bought it.
The dress what no longer fits but which felt important at the time you bought it.
The dress that no longer fits but that felt important at the time when you had bought it.
The dress which no longer fits but which it felt important at the time you bought it.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'That … but which…' correctly combines two relative clauses for a defining and a non-defining element.
mcQuestions.hint: The first relative pronoun defines the noun; 'which' can replace 'that' in the second clause.

options: gathered dust
collected dust
assembled dust
built dust
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Gather dust' is the fixed collocation meaning to be unused for a long time.
mcQuestions.hint: Only one verb forms a natural idiom with 'dust' to mean 'be neglected'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'could' correctly to express past ability?
options: Their families couldn't part with them, at least not immediately.
Their families couldn't to part with them, at least not immediately.
Their families couldn't parting with them, at least not immediately.
Their families couldn't parted with them, at least not immediately.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Could + bare infinitive' — no 'to', no '-ing', no past participle after 'could'.
mcQuestions.hint: Modal verbs take the bare infinitive directly — no extra 'to' or form change.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence has the most natural B1 structure for a concluding thought?
options: That, I think, is the clearest explanation of why we save things.
That I think is the clearest explanation, of why we save things.
That is, I think the clearest explanation of why we save things.
I think that the clearest is the explanation of why we save things.
mcQuestions.explanation: Fronting 'that' and inserting 'I think' as a parenthetical is a natural B1 way to introduce a conclusion.
mcQuestions.hint: A fronted demonstrative ('That') with a parenthetical opinion ('I think') makes the conclusion feel grounded.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option correctly uses a gerund as the subject of a sentence?
options: Throwing them away felt like throwing away the person.
To throw them away felt like to throw away the person.
Throw them away felt like throw away the person.
Throwing them away felt like to throw away the person.
mcQuestions.explanation: Gerunds ('-ing' forms) act as nouns and can be the subject of a sentence; 'like' is also followed by a gerund here.
mcQuestions.hint: After 'feel like', use a gerund, not an infinitive.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'not … but' to clarify motivation?
options: Not because we might use them, but because we're not yet ready to admit a chapter is closed.
Not because we might use them, but because we're not yet ready to admit a chapter has closed.
Not because we might use them, but because we're not yet ready to admitting a chapter is closed.
Not because we might to use them, but because we're not yet ready to admit a chapter is closed.
mcQuestions.explanation: Both options A and B are mostly correct; however, 'a chapter has closed' (present perfect) more naturally signals the completion of a phase in this context. Option C uses 'admitting' incorrectly after 'ready to'. Option D incorrectly uses 'might to use'.
mcQuestions.hint: After 'ready to', use the bare infinitive. 'Might' never takes 'to' before the following verb.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Is there a point at which keeping things is healthy — even useful — and how would you know when you've crossed from one side to the other?
